content: We can draw an analogy between the linguistic double meaning of "reflexion" (as both a technical agent framework and a general metacognitive act of critical reflection taught in high school literacy curricula) and the core challenge of modern LLM self-reflection mechanisms: the most effective multi-perspective LLM self-reflection benefits from framing the act as both a specialized technical memory routine and a generalizable literacy skill, just like how reflexion is taught in language classrooms. Treating LLM self-reflection as a teachable literacy skill rather than just a technical architecture allows us to import active reflection pedagogies from high school curricula to improve autonomous agent performance.
